title: My Day
text: Describe what you did today from morning to evening. Use simple past tense (I woke up, I ate, I went...). Try to write at least five sentences.
tips: Use time words: first, then, after that, finally.
Stick to the past tense for finished actions.
End with how your day made you feel.

title: Something I Am Grateful For
text: Write about one thing you are thankful for today and explain why it matters to you.
tips: Give a concrete example, not just a general statement.
Use "because" to explain your reasons.

title: A Small Win
text: Describe a small success you had recently. What did you do, what challenge did you overcome, and what did you learn?
tips: Show the obstacle before the success to make it interesting.
Use linking words: although, however, as a result.

title: The Unexpected Letter
text: You open your mailbox and find a letter with no name on it. Write what the letter says and what you do next.
tips: Set the scene in the first sentence.
Use dialogue with quotation marks to bring it to life.

title: The Door That Should Not Exist
text: While walking a familiar street, you notice a door you have never seen before. Tell the story of what happens when you open it.
tips: Build suspense — delay the reveal.
Appeal to the five senses to immerse the reader.
Vary sentence length to control pace.

title: An Unreliable Narrator
text: Write a short scene told by a narrator the reader slowly realises cannot be fully trusted. Let the truth leak through the cracks.
tips: Plant small contradictions in what the narrator claims.
Use subtext: what is NOT said can say the most.
Keep a consistent voice even as doubt grows.

title: Is Social Media Good or Bad?
text: State your opinion on whether social media has a positive or negative effect on society. Support it with at least two reasons and examples.
tips: Open with a clear thesis statement.
One reason per paragraph, each with an example.
Acknowledge the other side, then refute it.

title: Should Education Be Free?
text: Argue for or against making higher education free for everyone. Address counter-arguments and use evidence-based reasoning.
tips: Use a clear structure: claim, evidence, reasoning, link.
Employ academic connectors: furthermore, nevertheless, consequently.
Avoid emotional language; rely on logic.

title: My Favourite Place
text: Describe a place where you feel calm and happy. What do you see, hear, and smell there?
tips: Use adjectives, but choose precise ones.
Describe at least three different senses.

title: A Storm Arrives
text: Describe the moments before, during, and after a thunderstorm without ever using the word "storm".
tips: Use strong verbs instead of many adjectives.
Show change over time — before, during, after.
Try one metaphor or simile.

title: A Polite Email
text: Write a professional email to a teacher or manager asking to reschedule a meeting. Keep it polite, clear, and brief.
tips: Begin with a greeting and a clear subject line.
State your request early, then give the reason.
Close politely: "Thank you for your understanding."

title: A Cover Letter Paragraph
text: Write one strong paragraph for a job application explaining why you are a good fit for a role you want.
tips: Match your skills to the job, with one concrete achievement.
Use confident but not arrogant language.
Quantify results where you can (e.g. "improved X by 20%").

title: A Lesson From a Mistake
text: Think of a mistake you made. Describe it honestly and explain what it taught you.
tips: Be specific about what went wrong.
Focus on growth, not blame.

title: Who I Was Five Years Ago
text: Reflect on how you have changed over the past five years. What stayed the same, and what would surprise your past self?
tips: Contrast past and present clearly.
Use the present perfect: "I have learned...", "I have become...".
